title: Incident Command & Emergency Management (ICS/NIMS)
learningObjectives: Describe the purpose and core features of the Incident Command System (ICS).
Name the five major ICS functional sections and their responsibilities.
Explain span of control and modular expansion.
Define unified command and when it is used.
Describe NIMS and how it standardizes response across agencies.
Explain the hospital's version of ICS and the health professional's place in it.

summary.title: Why Incident Command Exists
summary.content: Large events involve many agencies — fire, EMS, police, public works, hospitals, public health — that must act as one organization. The Incident Command System (ICS) is a standardized, scalable management structure that provides common terminology, clear chain of command, defined roles, and integrated communications. It prevents the classic failure of disasters: multiple groups working hard at cross purposes.

summary.title: The Five Functional Sections
summary.content: ICS organizes work into five functions. Command sets objectives and holds overall authority; it includes the command staff — safety officer, public information officer, and liaison officer. Operations does the tactical work: rescue, triage, treatment, transport. Planning collects information, tracks resources, and writes the incident action plan. Logistics obtains everything the response needs — supplies, facilities, food, communications. Finance/Administration tracks costs, contracts, claims, and time records. Every task in a response lives somewhere in these five functions.

summary.title: Modular, Scalable, Span of Control
summary.content: ICS expands and contracts with the incident: a small event may need only an incident commander, while a catastrophe activates every section with branches, divisions, and groups. The guiding rule is span of control: one supervisor should directly manage roughly three to seven people (five is the classic optimum). When a supervisor's span grows too large, the structure subdivides.

summary.title: Unified Command and Transfer of Command
summary.content: When an incident crosses jurisdictions or disciplines — a hazmat spill involving fire, police, EMS, and environmental agencies — unified command lets the key agencies share command decisions while maintaining a single coordinated plan and one Operations section. Command transfers formally, with a briefing, when a more qualified or designated officer assumes it; everyone is told who now commands.

summary.title: NIMS: The National Framework
summary.content: The National Incident Management System (NIMS) is the U.S. doctrine that standardizes incident management across all levels of government and the private sector. It incorporates ICS, defines resource typing (so a requested 'strike team' means the same thing everywhere), credentialing, mutual aid, and emergency operations centers (EOCs) that support on-scene command. For health professionals, NIMS means your hospital's system plugs into the same structure the fire service uses.

summary.title: Hospital Incident Command (HICS)
summary.content: Hospitals use an ICS adaptation (commonly the Hospital Incident Command System, HICS) with the same five functions applied to facility problems: bed surge, staff callback, supply shortages, evacuation. Clinicians in a disaster work within assigned roles — often defined by job action sheets — reporting through the structure rather than to their everyday supervisors. Knowing your facility's command structure before the event is a core BDLS competency.

clinicalPearls: In ICS you report to your incident supervisor, not your everyday boss — one person, one supervisor.
Span of control 3–7 (optimally ~5): when a leader has more direct reports than that, the structure should subdivide.
The safety officer can halt any operation immediately — the one role with that authority besides command.
Job action sheets convert your clinical skills into a defined incident role; read yours before the disaster.
redFlags: Multiple agencies each running their own separate command posts for the same incident.
A supervisor directly managing 15 people — span of control has collapsed.
Staff bypassing the command structure to act on hallway instructions.
commonMistakes: Confusing the incident commander (on-scene authority) with the EOC (off-scene support and coordination).
Assuming ICS is only for fire services — it is designed for all disciplines including hospitals.
Forgetting the formal briefing when command transfers.
memoryAids: Five functions — C-O-P-L-F: Command, Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin.
'Command thinks, Operations does, Planning tracks, Logistics gets, Finance pays.'
Span of control: one hand — about five fingers, about five reports.

decisionTrees.title: Fitting Into Incident Command as a Health Professional
decisionTrees.content:
Disaster activation announced
  → Report to your designated check-in point (never self-dispatch)
  → Receive assignment + job action sheet
  → Identify YOUR incident supervisor (one person)
  → Perform assigned role; communicate up the chain
     → Task outside your competence? Say so — constructive refusal is safety
     → New hazard or resource need? Report to your supervisor
  → At shift end: formal handoff + demobilization through the system

references: NDLSF BDLS v4.0 — Incident Command and Emergency Management Systems
FEMA — NIMS and ICS doctrine (as taught in BDLS v4.0)
